Metadata.description: {name} — university-level CS teaching (algorithms, data structures, databases) and hands-on full-stack engineering. Work history and public profiles linked on-site.

top.title: Intro
top.body: Who I am, how I work with teams and stakeholders, and the stack I use to ship reliable full-stack systems.

subjects.title: Teaching focus
subjects.body: Core modules—algorithms, data structures, and databases—framed for engineers who need durable intuition, not cramming.

Hero.bio: I teach algorithms, data structures, and databases at university level, and I build the same kind of systems I talk about in class: APIs, integrations, and operational concerns included—not slide-deck-only examples.
Hero.detail: Recent work spans scalable services, OAuth and enterprise SSO, Jira and third-party APIs, and real-time interfaces where latency matters. In the classroom I emphasize measurable trade-offs, testing, and architectures (including hexagonal and event-driven) that survive contact with production.
Hero.proofHint: These links go to the public profiles I use professionally. They match the employment and education timeline in the experience section below.

TechnologiesSection.heading: Technologies
TechnologiesSection.lead: Stacks from real delivery work and from what I assign in coursework—grouped so you can scan without marketing fluff.

Subjects.heading: Core teaching areas
Subjects.lead: Core university modules with runnable exercises—not slide-only summaries. Open a subject for topics, outcomes, and how sessions are structured.

algorithms.title: Algorithms
algorithms.summary: Complexity, correctness, and the patterns that keep large systems predictable—from sorting and search to graphs and dynamic programming.
algorithms.overview:
We build **intuition for cost models** (time and space), learn to argue **correctness**, and practice choosing structures and algorithms that match real constraints—not textbook trivia.

Labs stress **implementation + analysis**: tracing invariants, benchmarking, and spotting when an asymptotic win is drowned out by constants or I/O.
highlights: Sorting, searching, and selection on arrays and trees
Graph representations, traversals, shortest paths, spanning trees
Greedy, divide & conquer, and dynamic programming patterns
Heuristics for interviews and production debugging
outcomes: Estimate and compare algorithmic costs for non-trivial inputs
Select appropriate data structures for a given access pattern
Implement core algorithms with clear invariants and tests
Communicate trade-offs to peers and stakeholders

data-structures.title: Data structures
data-structures.summary: How memory layout, access patterns, and persistence choices shape performance—from arrays and lists to trees, heaps, and hash-based designs.
data-structures.overview:
We connect **abstract interfaces** (list, map, priority queue) to **concrete representations** and the hardware realities they imply: cache locality, allocation churn, and iteration cost.

Where useful we touch **on-disk** structures at a high level (B-trees, LSM ideas) so database modules later feel grounded.
highlights: Arrays, linked structures, stacks, queues, deques
Hash tables: collisions, load factor, when they fail gracefully
Amortized analysis and when to trust it
Choosing structures for APIs you design yourself
outcomes: Match structure choice to read/write/iterate workloads
Reason about worst, average, and amortized costs
Implement and test core structures from scratch
Read real-world stack traces and performance profiles with structure in mind

databases.title: Databases
databases.summary: Relational modeling, SQL fluency, transactions, and how storage engines and indexes behave under load.
databases.overview:
We treat the database as a **system**, not a black box: schemas and constraints, query planning basics, isolation levels, and the failure modes teams hit in production.

Exercises blend **SQL** with **schema design** and **operational hygiene** (migrations, observability, safe rollout patterns).
highlights: Relational modeling, normalization, and pragmatic denormalization
SQL: joins, aggregates, subqueries, window functions (intro)
Transactions, isolation, locks, and common anomalies
Indexes: B-tree intuition, covering indexes, EXPLAIN-oriented tuning
Migrations, backups, and basic replication concepts
outcomes: Design schemas that enforce invariants at the right layer
Write and refactor SQL for clarity and predictable plans
Choose isolation strategies for a given consistency need
Collaborate with backend teams on performance and migration safety

Courseworks.heading: Courseworks
Courseworks.lead: Active project lines you can fork, break, and rebuild—each with a dedicated deep-dive and learning objectives.

hexagonal-order-service.title: Hexagonal order service
hexagonal-order-service.excerpt: A teaching service that keeps business rules in the center while HTTP, SQL, and messaging stay at the edges.
hexagonal-order-service.overview:
Students implement an **order-taking flow** where the domain never imports framework code. **Ports** describe use cases; **adapters** translate to HTTP handlers, repositories, and message publishers.

The goal is fluency in **dependency direction**, **fakes in tests**, and **swap-friendly infrastructure**—the same skills that keep large codebases refactorable.
objectives: Separate domain logic from transport and persistence adapters
Design ports that stay stable as infrastructure changes
Test use cases with in-memory fakes before wiring real IO
Document boundaries with OpenAPI and clear module ownership
hexagonal-order-service.practicePrompt: Sketch the place-order port and two adapters (HTTP + Postgres) before writing any framework glue. List three invariants the domain must enforce even if the database is swapped.

event-driven-inventory.title: Event-driven inventory
event-driven-inventory.excerpt: Inventory changes modeled as events—consumers, retries, and exactly-once illusions done responsibly.
event-driven-inventory.overview:
We walk a **stock movement pipeline** where multiple services react to the same facts. Students implement **idempotent consumers**, **versioned events**, and a **read model projector** that can be rebuilt.

Emphasis is on **operational reality**: at-least-once delivery, poison messages, observability, and backpressure—not just happy-path diagrams.
objectives: Model domain events with explicit schemas and versioning
Implement safe retries and idempotency keys
Build and rebuild projections from an event log
Instrument consumers for lag, failures, and poison queues
event-driven-inventory.practicePrompt: Pick one inventory adjustment and write the event schema (JSON or Avro-style). Then list every consumer that must react and what happens if the same event is delivered twice.

fullstack-assessment-platform.title: Full-stack assessment platform
fullstack-assessment-platform.tagline: BFF, async grading, and observable pipelines
fullstack-assessment-platform.excerpt: Submit answers fast, grade in the background, and keep the UI honest about progress—Next.js plus a worker-friendly backend.
fullstack-assessment-platform.overview:
The UI collects submissions while **grading jobs** run asynchronously. Students implement a **BFF** that validates and enqueues work, workers that grade deterministically, and **telemetry** that makes bottlenecks visible.

We stress **API contracts**, **status endpoints**, and **user-visible state machines** so partial failures are understandable.
objectives: Design APIs that separate submission from long-running work
Implement workers with retries, dead-letter handling, and metrics
Expose honest progress and error states in the UI
Trace requests across services with correlation IDs
fullstack-assessment-platform.practicePrompt: Map the submit → enqueue → grade → notify sequence. Identify three failure points and how the UI should represent each without lying about completion.

Projects.heading: Personal projects
Projects.lead: Side work and tooling that feeds the curriculum—distinct from formal courseworks, but often reused in exercises.

arch-kata-cli.title: Architecture kata CLI
arch-kata-cli.description: A small CLI for running repeatable architecture katas—constraints, timers, and lightweight scoring for workshop groups.

lecture-notes-publisher.title: Lecture notes publisher
lecture-notes-publisher.description: Next.js + MDX pipeline for publishing course notes with previews, syntax highlighting, and export-friendly layouts.

latency-lab.title: Latency lab
latency-lab.description: Dockerized playground for tracing, metrics, and comparing latency under synthetic load—used to teach observability basics.

WorkHistory.heading: Work history
WorkHistory.lead: By calendar year: expand a year for roles, tech stack, and bullets aligned with {name}'s résumé. Degrees and language levels are listed after employment.
